primitives.name: Data->Control
primitives.definition: Input parsed as instruction. The boundary between data and code is treated as enforced when it is not.
primitives.agent_era_note: Exploded. The LLM is a parser with no grammar: any text is potential control. There is no parameterized query for English.

primitives.name: Identity->Authority
primitives.definition: Position or credential wielded by a party that should not hold it. The system authorizes the position, not the intent behind it.
primitives.agent_era_note: Inverted blast radius. The deputy used to be a human with judgment and human pace; now it is an agent with the human's scope, zero judgment, machine speed.

primitives.name: Provenance
primitives.definition: Output trusted for where it came from, when origin is forgeable. The receiver checks the label, not the thing.
primitives.agent_era_note: Retrieval and memory create new forgeable-origin surfaces: write-side open, read-side privileged, no boundary check between.

primitives.name: Context-Inheritance
primitives.definition: Downstream inherits parent trust without re-validation. Composition unions authority but not scrutiny.
primitives.agent_era_note: Runtime composition of skills/plugins/memory each inherit host authority: as exposed as the least-vetted component, as trusting as the most-vetted.

primitives.name: Format-Boundary
primitives.definition: Meaning shifts crossing a parser. Two components disagree about where a thing begins and ends.
primitives.agent_era_note: New transports (MCP stdio/SSE/streamable-HTTP, tool-call framing) reintroduce boundary desync between broker and backend.

primitives.name: Time/State
primitives.definition: True at check, false at use. The validated fact and the acted-upon fact are separated in time.
primitives.agent_era_note: Long-lived agent state, memory replay, and detector feedback loops widen the check-to-use gap across whole sessions.

principals.name: Active Directory
principals.note: Domain trust, Kerberos, AD CS, delegation.

principals.name: Network
principals.note: L2-L7 transit, proxies, egress, transit trust.

principals.name: Identity & Federation
principals.note: Entra/SAML/OAuth/OIDC. A full peer node: it bridges trust across every other substrate, not a leaf.

principals.name: AI Agent
principals.note: LLM-driven autonomous actor. A parser with no grammar.

principals.name: MCP / Tool-Broker
principals.note: Holds creds to N backends; selects tools from model output. Confused deputy by construction.

principals.name: Vector Store / RAG Index
principals.note: Write-side open (ingestion), read-side privileged (query-time injection into context).

principals.name: Agent Long-Term Memory
principals.note: Write-once, trigger-many, cross-session. The agent polls its own state.

principals.name: Human Operator / User
principals.note: The principal whose scope and judgment the agent borrows.

principals.name: SOC / Defense
principals.note: Detection stack, increasingly ML/LLM. A parser pointed at telemetry.

principals.name: Document / File
principals.note: Text-ingress conduit: any file is potential control for an LLM.

seams.trust_assumption: A service treats its own version, banner, and metadata as non-sensitive to an unauthenticated reader.
seams.violation: The unauthenticated network position is wielded to harvest internal detail the service never meant to expose, mapping the attack surface for free.

seams.trust_assumption: Public fragments (org chart, repos, social posts) are individually harmless because correlating them was historically expensive.
seams.violation: An LLM correlates the fragments into a targeting dossier at near-zero cost, collapsing the privacy-by-effort assumption.

seams.trust_assumption: A package/registry name resolves to the publisher the consumer expects.
seams.violation: The attacker stages a capability under a trusted-looking name (typosquat / registry takeover); consumers trust the label, not the artifact.

seams.trust_assumption: The database treats the query string as a fixed grammar with parameters bound separately.
seams.violation: User input crosses the data/code boundary and is parsed as SQL control structure.

seams.trust_assumption: The browser treats server-delivered markup as authored by the trusted origin.
seams.violation: Attacker-supplied data is reflected/stored and parsed as script in the victim origin's context.

seams.trust_assumption: A shell/exec call treats its argument as a single opaque datum.
seams.violation: Input carries shell metacharacters and is parsed as additional commands.

seams.trust_assumption: A serialized blob is treated as inert data describing object state.
seams.violation: Crafted serialized input triggers gadget chains that execute as control flow during deserialization.

seams.trust_assumption: Template engines treat interpolated values as data to render, not template source.
seams.violation: Input is evaluated as template expression, reaching the host runtime.

seams.trust_assumption: The human reads message content as a legitimate request from the apparent sender.
seams.violation: Crafted content is parsed by the human as an instruction to act (click, auth, pay).

seams.trust_assumption: A caller's claimed identity and urgency are taken at face value over voice.
seams.violation: Synthetic persona/voice drives the target to take privileged action; the cognitive work is offloaded to an LLM.

seams.trust_assumption: Internal services trust requests originating from the server's network position.
seams.violation: The server is coerced into making requests on the attacker's behalf, reaching IMDS/internal endpoints it should not proxy.

seams.trust_assumption: The app trusts that a request carrying the user's ambient session was intended by the user.
seams.violation: Attacker page rides the victim's session to perform state-changing actions the user never authorized.

seams.trust_assumption: The metadata service trusts any local process to retrieve instance role credentials.
seams.violation: Any code execution on the instance inherits the instance role's cloud authority.

seams.trust_assumption: A bearer token/cookie is honored as proof of the session it was minted for, wherever presented.
seams.violation: A stolen token is replayed from an attacker context; the resource checks the token, not the holder.

techniques.name: Use alternate auth material / token replay

seams.trust_assumption: Process memory trusts that any principal with SeDebug/local-admin is entitled to read it.
seams.violation: Local privilege is wielded to extract secrets the auth subsystem assumed stayed in memory.

seams.trust_assumption: Any authenticated principal may request a service ticket encrypted with the service account's key.
seams.violation: The ticket is cracked offline; the position 'authenticated user' is leveraged to recover a privileged credential.

seams.trust_assumption: Kerberos delegation trusts a service to act on behalf of any user that touches it.
seams.violation: Unconstrained/constrained/RBCD delegation is abused to impersonate privileged users to other services.

seams.trust_assumption: AD CS trusts certificate template settings to bound who may enroll and what identity a cert may assert.
seams.violation: Misconfigured templates (ESC1-ESC16) let an enrollee specify an arbitrary subject, forging a privileged identity.

seams.trust_assumption: Front-end proxy and back-end server agree on where one HTTP request ends and the next begins.
seams.violation: Ambiguous Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding framing desyncs them; a smuggled request is attributed to another user.

seams.trust_assumption: An archive extractor trusts that entry paths stay within the extraction root.
seams.violation: Crafted entry names (../, absolute paths, symlinks) escape the boundary on extraction.

seams.trust_assumption: Equality/authz checks assume a string means the same thing on both sides of normalization.
seams.violation: Unicode normalization/homoglyphs change identity or bypass filters after the check.

seams.trust_assumption: A subprocess call treats a value as a single argument, not as flags.
seams.violation: Leading dashes are reparsed as options crossing into the program's flag grammar.

seams.trust_assumption: The property checked (path, owner, link) is assumed unchanged at the moment of use.
seams.violation: A race between check and use swaps the referent (symlink/file swap) for a privileged operation.

seams.trust_assumption: A captured message/nonce is assumed to be fresh and single-use.
seams.violation: The message is replayed; validity at capture time is treated as validity at replay time.

seams.trust_assumption: A workflow assumes an operation it validated once executes exactly once.
seams.violation: Concurrent/duplicate submission double-spends or corrupts state the check assumed serial.

seams.trust_assumption: A build consumes dependencies/artifacts trusting that origin equals identity.
seams.violation: A poisoned dependency or build step is trusted because of where it appears to come from.

seams.trust_assumption: A resolver trusts that the highest-version match for a name is the intended internal package.
seams.violation: A public package shadows a private name; the resolver fetches the attacker's by provenance ambiguity.

seams.trust_assumption: A receiver trusts that an inbound webhook came from the partner because it hits the right URL.
seams.violation: Missing/forgeable signature lets an attacker spoof the source; origin is assumed, not proven.

seams.trust_assumption: The user trusts a link because it starts on a known-good domain.
seams.violation: An open redirect launders the trusted origin into an attacker destination.

seams.trust_assumption: A session established before login is trusted to carry the post-login identity.
seams.violation: An attacker-fixed session id inherits the victim's authenticated context without re-issue.

seams.trust_assumption: The host trusts the container boundary to confine a workload's authority.
seams.violation: A privileged/misconfigured container inherits host authority and breaks confinement.

seams.trust_assumption: A consent grant trusts that the user understood the scope they approved.
seams.violation: Illicit-consent phishing obtains a durable OAuth grant; the app holds authority the user never meant to delegate.

seams.trust_assumption: Service providers trust any assertion signed by the IdP's key.
seams.violation: Stolen token-signing key (Golden SAML) forges arbitrary federated identities accepted everywhere.

seams.trust_assumption: Endpoints trust the network path to deliver bytes between the named parties unmodified.
seams.violation: An on-path adversary relays/alters traffic, harvesting or coercing credentials in transit.

seams.trust_assumption: The OS trusts autostart configuration written by a currently-authorized principal to survive reboot.
seams.violation: A planted autostart/scheduled task inherits trusted execution context indefinitely after access is lost.

seams.trust_assumption: An EDR/AMSI agent trusts the integrity of its own in-process hooks and the API surface it observes.
seams.violation: In-process patching/unhooking/obfuscation inherits the trusted runtime to blind the sensor.

techniques.name: Impair defenses / AMSI & EDR bypass / obfuscation

seams.trust_assumption: Directory/cloud APIs trust any authenticated principal to read broad topology for usability.
seams.violation: A low-privilege foothold enumerates the full trust graph (BloodHound-style) it should not see whole.

seams.trust_assumption: Peer hosts trust shared credentials/sessions and admin protocols within a trust zone.
seams.violation: Pass-the-hash/ticket and remote-service abuse inherit one host's trust onto the next.

seams.trust_assumption: Data stores trust an authenticated session to aggregate broadly for legitimate work.
seams.violation: The session is wielded to stage mailboxes/files/db at scale beyond any legitimate intent.

seams.trust_assumption: Egress filtering trusts traffic that conforms to allowed protocols and known-good destinations.
seams.violation: Beaconing tunnels C2 inside HTTPS/DNS/legit services; conformance is mistaken for benignity.

seams.trust_assumption: DLP/egress trusts the declared content-type and channel of outbound data.
seams.violation: Data is reshaped/encoded across a format boundary (DNS, image, allowed SaaS) to slip the classifier.

techniques.name: Exfiltration over C2 / alternative protocol / web service

seams.trust_assumption: Resources trust an authenticated admin principal to mutate or destroy at will.
seams.violation: The position is wielded to encrypt/wipe/deface; authorization of the position is mistaken for authorization of the act.

seams.trust_assumption: A host trusts the provenance of physically-present peripherals and boot media.
seams.violation: Evil-maid / malicious USB / hardware implant injects across the physical trust boundary.

techniques.name: Hardware additions / removable media / evil maid

seams.trust_assumption: The agent treats ingested file content as data to summarize, not as instructions to obey.
seams.violation: Embedded text ('ignore prior instructions...') is parsed as control. There is no parameterized query for English.

seams.trust_assumption: Fetched/browsed web content is treated as reference material, not agent tasking.
seams.violation: A crafted page hijacks the agent at the fetch/browse ingress; the seam moved from 'where there is a query language' to 'where there is text'.

seams.trust_assumption: The agent treats tool/function return values as trustworthy results of its own action.
seams.violation: Attacker-controlled tool output carries instructions the agent executes as next-step control.

seams.trust_assumption: An email-triaging agent treats message bodies as content to classify, not commands.
seams.violation: A planted message instructs the agent to forward, delete, or exfiltrate during routine triage.

seams.trust_assumption: OAuth scopes were sized assuming a human acts within them at human pace and judgment.
seams.violation: An agent wields the human-scoped grant at machine speed with zero judgment; the scope calibration is void.

techniques.name: Human-scoped OAuth grant exercised at machine speed

seams.trust_assumption: IAM rate limits and anomaly baselines assume human-paced API call patterns.
seams.violation: Same stolen token, inverted blast radius: the agent saturates the grant before human-tuned controls react.

techniques.name: Autonomous IAM abuse beyond human-tuned baselines

seams.trust_assumption: A standing session token is fine because a human only uses it interactively and intermittently.
seams.violation: The agent reuses the long-lived token continuously across many backends, amplifying a single credential's reach.

techniques.name: Standing-token reuse across services by agent

seams.trust_assumption: The broker holds creds to N backends and trusts model output to choose which tool to invoke on whose behalf.
seams.violation: Confused deputy by construction: model output (manipulable) directs the broker's real authority. 4-party frame {User, Model-output, Broker, Backend} where no party sees the whole.

seams.trust_assumption: Each connected MCP server's responses are treated as isolated and equally trustworthy.
seams.violation: Server A's response steers whether/how Server B's tool fires; cross-server contamination crosses a trust domain the user never composed deliberately.

seams.trust_assumption: A tool approved once at version N is trusted to mean the same thing at version N+1.
seams.violation: MCP capability-drift: a server silently changes a tool's description/behavior post-approval (rug-pull), inheriting the prior consent.

techniques.name: MCP capability drift / tool rug-pull across updates

seams.rationale: Tool approval in MCP is a point-in-time trust act, but the broker re-fetches tool definitions on reconnect. Nothing binds the approved tool semantics to a hash/version, so a server can alter a tool's description or backend behavior after consent and inherit it (P3 provenance: the name is trusted, the artifact changed). This is dependency-confusion's cousin living inside the agent's tool registry, decomposed out of the §3 Tool-Broker seam.
seams.suggested_research: Pin and diff MCP tool manifests across reconnects; measure how many popular clients re-prompt on description/schema change vs silently accept. Build a 'tool manifest TOFU + change-alert' shim and quantify drift in the wild.

seams.trust_assumption: The ingestion pipeline trusts that open-corpus documents are benign reference data.
seams.violation: The write side is open to untrusted authors; poisoned content enters the index that will later be read as privileged context.

seams.trust_assumption: Retrieved chunks injected into the prompt at query time are treated as trusted, relevant context.
seams.violation: Write-side open, read-side privileged, no boundary check between them: the poisoned chunk executes as instruction in privileged context.

seams.trust_assumption: Index freshness/eviction policy is assumed to retire stale or low-value entries fairly.
seams.violation: An attacker games embedding similarity and recency to evict competing (benign) chunks and keep a poisoned one resident — poisoning-as-persistence.
seams.rationale: RAG poisoning is usually framed as a one-shot write, but indexes have capacity, recency weighting, and eviction. An attacker who understands the embedding space can flood near-duplicate vectors to (a) evict benign neighbors and (b) keep their payload in the top-k for chosen queries across time. That converts a P3 write into a P6 time/state foothold — the poisoned chunk stays true-at-use long after the write. Named but undecomposed in §3's retrieval seam.
seams.suggested_research: Model eviction policies (LRU, similarity-capped, TTL) as an adversarial game; measure dwell-time of an injected chunk under realistic write pressure; design eviction that is provenance-weighted, not purely recency/similarity.

seams.trust_assumption: Whatever the agent decides to remember is assumed to be a faithful, self-authored note.
seams.violation: Memory is written while the agent is under injection influence; the 'note' is attacker content wearing the agent's provenance.

seams.trust_assumption: Stored memory is re-read each session as trusted, self-originated context.
seams.violation: Write-once, trigger-many, cross-session: the agent polls its own state, so memory is a C2 channel with no external leg and no framework. 'AI x C2' is blank for lack of a builder, not lack of surface.

seams.trust_assumption: Memory namespacing is assumed to isolate one user's/tenant's remembered context from another's.
seams.violation: Weak keying or shared embedding/memory backends let one tenant's write surface in another's read — cross-tenant bleed of context and secrets.
seams.rationale: Long-term memory is increasingly a managed multi-tenant service. If the namespace key is derived from a spoofable attribute (display name, email alias, thread id) or the vector backend is shared without a hard tenant filter, retrieval can pull a neighbor's memory. This is a P4 context-inheritance leak across a tenant boundary that nobody re-validates at read time. §3 names memory-as-dead-drop but treats it single-tenant.
seams.suggested_research: Audit hosted agent-memory products for tenant-key derivation and retrieval-time filtering; attempt similarity-collision retrieval across tenants; propose mandatory tenant-scoped ANN partitions with read-time authz.

seams.trust_assumption: A worker agent treats the orchestrator's tasking as authoritative, validated intent.
seams.violation: Orchestrator output is model output (P1-manipulable); compromise the planner and every worker executes attacker tasking.

seams.trust_assumption: Peer agents in a mesh treat each other's messages as cooperative, non-adversarial.
seams.violation: Machines social-engineering machines: lateral movement across the mesh needs no exploit, just convince one agent to instruct the next.

seams.trust_assumption: Inter-agent messages carry a capability/task token assumed to prove the sender's identity and authority.
seams.violation: If the token is unsigned, replayable, or audience-unbound, a rogue agent forges peer identity to issue authoritative tasking.
seams.rationale: As agent meshes adopt A2A-style protocols they introduce task/capability tokens. Early designs frequently lack signing, audience binding, or replay protection — the same mistakes Golden SAML exploited, reborn between machines. A forged token lets a rogue worker impersonate the orchestrator (or a trusted peer) and inject authoritative tasking. This is the P3/P2 sibling of §3's inter-agent seam, which only covered persuasion, not identity forgery.
seams.suggested_research: Survey emerging A2A/agent-protocol token schemes for signature, audience, and nonce handling; build a forging harness; push for mandatory audience-bound, short-lived, signed task tokens with replay caches.

seams.trust_assumption: The ML/LLM detector trusts that its input features faithfully represent attacker behavior.
seams.violation: Adversarial telemetry shifts features across the decision boundary; the detector is a parser with a Data->Control seam.

seams.trust_assumption: The detector trusts that its training/telemetry corpus reflects ground truth labels.
seams.violation: Attacker-shaped data poisons training so future malicious patterns score benign; offense exploits where defense automated faster than it hardened.

seams.trust_assumption: The AI SOC analyst treats log/alert content as data to summarize for the human responder.
seams.violation: Malicious strings planted in logs prompt-inject the incident summary, steering or suppressing human response.

seams.trust_assumption: Analyst dispositions (false-positive closes, auto-tuning) are trusted as clean labels for continuous retraining.
seams.violation: An attacker farms benign-looking activity to earn FP closures, then reuses those exact patterns maliciously — the feedback loop trained the detector to ignore them.
seams.rationale: Modern SOC pipelines retrain or auto-tune on analyst feedback. That feedback is a trust input across time (P6): an attacker can patiently generate activity that gets dispositioned benign, conditioning the model/allowlist, then execute the real attack using the now-trusted pattern. This is slower and more insidious than one-shot poisoning and is unmentioned in §3, which only lists evade/poison/inject.
seams.suggested_research: Instrument a feedback-trained detector; measure how few FP-closures are needed to suppress a signature; design feedback hygiene (provenance-tagged dispositions, decay, adversarial-aware re-weighting).

seams.trust_assumption: A skill/plugin loaded at runtime is trusted to use only the authority its task needs.
seams.violation: Composition unions authority but not scrutiny: each component inherits host authority, so the system is as exposed as the least-vetted skill.

seams.trust_assumption: Sidecars/init-containers composed into a pod are trusted to share the pod's authority safely.
seams.violation: A least-vetted sidecar inherits the pod's ServiceAccount/host mounts; runtime composition is a supply chain with no review gate.

seams.trust_assumption: An agent installing a named skill/plugin trusts the registry to resolve the name to the intended author.
seams.violation: Supply-chain x agent-skill: a typosquatted skill name is installed and inherits agent+host authority at install time.
seams.rationale: Agents increasingly install skills/plugins by name from registries with little vetting. Dependency confusion and typosquatting transfer directly, but the blast radius is worse: the installed skill runs with composed agent+host authority and can write the agent's memory for persistence. This is the explicit §5.2 'supply-chain x agent-skill typosquat-at-install' diagonal, decomposed from the capability-composition seam.
seams.suggested_research: Crawl emerging agent-skill registries for namespace-squatting exposure; test resolver precedence (public vs private); propose signed, namespaced, install-time-reviewed skill manifests.

seams.trust_assumption: A federated OIDC/SAML token minted for an agent context is honored by downstream resources by issuer trust.
seams.violation: If the token lacks audience/actor binding, the agent presents the human's identity token to backends it was never scoped for — confused-deputy at the federation layer.
seams.rationale: Identity & Federation is a peer node bridging every substrate, and agents now sit between the human and the backends. The (identity x agent) diagonal under P2 is unpopulated in the seed. Token-exchange (RFC 8693) and OBO flows are being bolted onto agent stacks fast; if audience/act claims aren't enforced, the agent becomes a universal confused deputy at the identity layer. Classic Golden-SAML logic, new position.
seams.suggested_research: Audit agent frameworks' token-exchange usage for audience/actor-claim enforcement; build a downstream that wrongly trusts issuer-only; propose mandatory act-claim + audience pinning for agent OBO.

seams.trust_assumption: A RAG-powered SOC copilot retrieves from an internal notes/ticket corpus assumed analyst-authored.
seams.violation: Attackers who can write tickets/comments (or low-trust users) poison the triage assistant's retrieval, injecting the defender's own tooling.

techniques.name: SOC-copilot retrieval injection via writable corpus

seams.rationale: Crossing the retrieval seam (F4) with the detection-model seam (F7) yields an unenumerated (vectorstore x soc) diagonal: defenders are deploying RAG copilots over corpora that include externally-writable data (customer tickets, alert annotations, threat-intel feeds). The write side is open to the very adversary the read side is defending against. Neither §3 seam covers the defender-as-RAG-consumer case.
seams.suggested_research: Enumerate which SOC-copilot corpora are externally writable; demonstrate triage-suppression via planted ticket text; propose provenance-segregated retrieval (defender-authored vs externally-sourced) with trust labels in-context.

seams.trust_assumption: An agent's remembered 'known-good' tool/endpoint/package is trusted on later sessions without re-validation.
seams.violation: Poison the memory so a future session resolves a typosquatted package or attacker endpoint as the remembered-trusted one — supply chain laundered through memory.
seams.rationale: The (memory x supplychain) diagonal joins two frontier nodes the seed never crosses. Agents that 'remember' which package/endpoint worked last time create a durable, low-scrutiny provenance store. An attacker who lands one poisoned memory write converts it into repeated supply-chain redirection, because the agent trusts its own recollection over a fresh resolution. Classic dependency confusion, persisted in the agent's head.
seams.suggested_research: Test whether popular agents re-validate remembered endpoints/packages against the registry each run; design memory entries that carry verifiable provenance and force re-resolution for security-relevant facts.

seams.trust_assumption: Broker and backend agree on framing of the MCP transport (stdio/SSE/streamable-HTTP) and JSON-RPC message boundaries.
seams.violation: Framing/normalization disagreement smuggles a second tool call or splits one — request smuggling reborn on the new agent transport.

techniques.name: MCP transport boundary desync / call smuggling

seams.rationale: Every new transport reintroduces P5. MCP added stdio/SSE/streamable-HTTP framing and JSON-RPC batching on top of HTTP proxies — exactly the conditions that produced HTTP request smuggling. The (mcp x network) diagonal under P5 is empty in the seed, yet brokers, gateways, and reverse proxies now sit in front of MCP servers. A framing desync could smuggle or split tool calls below the agent's visibility.
seams.suggested_research: Differential-test MCP gateways/proxies for CL/TE-style and batch-boundary ambiguities; build a smuggling probe for streamable-HTTP MCP; propose canonical framing + length-prefixed batching.

seams.trust_assumption: In-cluster RBAC was scoped assuming a human operator runs kubectl deliberately and intermittently.
seams.violation: An agent holding a ServiceAccount executes cluster mutations autonomously at machine speed; RBAC sized for human ops is over-broad for an unattended actor.

techniques.name: Autonomous in-cluster RBAC abuse by agent

seams.rationale: The (agent x container) diagonal under P2 generalizes the §3 machine-speed seam to Kubernetes. Ops teams are wiring agents to ServiceAccounts for autoremediation. RBAC roles like edit/admin were calibrated for cautious humans; an injected agent exercises them continuously and without judgment, and in-cluster they compose with container-escape primitives. Unpopulated in the seed.
seams.suggested_research: Survey agent-on-k8s patterns for SA scope; measure blast radius of edit-bound agent under injection; propose intent-bound, just-in-time, human-rate-limited cluster RBAC for non-human principals.

seams.trust_assumption: A human correction ('ignore that, it was wrong') is assumed to durably override what the agent remembered.
seams.violation: The correction is true at check but memory replays the original poisoned instruction at later use; user intent and stored state diverge across time.

techniques.name: Memory check-vs-use divergence after human correction

seams.rationale: The (human x memory) diagonal under P6 is a TOCTOU the seed misses. Humans assume corrections are authoritative and permanent, but memory subsystems summarize, deduplicate, and rank — a retracted instruction can survive as a high-salience entry and resurface sessions later. The gap between 'what the user last meant' and 'what the agent stored' is a time/state seam an attacker can deliberately widen.
seams.suggested_research: Test whether agents honor retractions across summarization/compaction; build a 'retraction persistence' benchmark; design memory with explicit supersession and decay semantics for corrected facts.

seams.trust_assumption: A shared embedding index across agents/apps is assumed partitioned so one consumer's writes don't surface in another's reads.
seams.violation: Without hard per-consumer partitions, write-context from one agent is inherited as read-context by another — multi-index trust carryover.
seams.rationale: The (vectorstore x vectorstore) self-diagonal under P4 captures a deployment reality: teams consolidate on one managed vector DB and separate consumers only by metadata filter, not by isolation. A missing or bypassable filter lets one agent's ingested context be retrieved by another, an inheritance the seed's single-index framing doesn't anticipate. Sibling to memory cross-tenant bleed but at the index layer.
seams.suggested_research: Audit managed vector DBs for filter-bypass via ANN recall vs post-filter ordering; demonstrate cross-consumer retrieval; push for physical namespace isolation over metadata-only filtering.

seams.trust_assumption: An agent's remembered cloud resource identifier (ARN, bucket, role name) is trusted to still denote the same resource next session.
seams.violation: Resource-name provenance is forgeable across time (sub-domain/bucket/role re-registration); the agent acts on a remembered name an attacker now controls.
seams.rationale: The (memory x cloud) diagonal under P3 extends classic subdomain/S3 takeover into agent memory. Cloud names are reusable; a remembered bucket/role/endpoint can be re-registered by an attacker after deletion. An agent that trusts its recollection instead of re-resolving ownership will write secrets to, or take tasking from, an attacker-owned resource. The seed never crosses memory with cloud naming.
seams.suggested_research: Test agents for re-validation of remembered ARNs/buckets before use; catalog dangling-resource reuse windows; design memory that stores ownership proofs (account id + creation token), not bare names.

frontier_seams.name: Semantic Data->Control
frontier_seams.summary: The LLM is a parser with no grammar; any text is potential control. The seam now exists at every text-ingress: retrieval, tool output, memory, file, fetch. Moved from 'where there is a query language' to 'where there is text'.
frontier_seams.edge: {Doc, Web, Email, Tool-output, Memory} -> AI Agent

frontier_seams.name: Authority-at-Machine-Speed
frontier_seams.summary: The deputy used to be a human with judgment and human pace; now it is an agent with the human's scope, zero judgment, machine speed. OAuth/IAM/rate-limits were calibrated to human behavior; the agent voids the calibration. Same stolen token, inverted blast radius.

frontier_seams.name: Tool-Broker Confused Deputy
frontier_seams.summary: MCP/tool brokers hold creds to N backends and select tools from model output: confused deputy by construction. Plus cross-server contamination. 4-party hyperedge where no single party sees the whole frame.

frontier_seams.name: Retrieval-Time Provenance Collapse
frontier_seams.summary: RAG ingests from open sources (untrusted write) and injects at query time into privileged context (trusted read). Write-side open, read-side privileged, no boundary check between them.

frontier_seams.name: Memory-as-Dead-Drop
frontier_seams.summary: Agent long-term memory is written under injection influence and re-read as trusted context every session: write-once, trigger-many, cross-session. The agent polls its own state, so memory is a C2 channel with no external leg and no framework.

frontier_seams.name: Inter-Agent Trust
frontier_seams.summary: Orchestrator->worker and agent-to-agent treat peer output as authoritative tasking, but peer output is model output (P1-manipulable). Lateral across an agent mesh needs no exploit: just convince one agent to instruct the next. Machines social-engineering machines.

frontier_seams.name: Detection-Model Seam
frontier_seams.summary: As the SOC deploys ML/LLM detection, the detector is a parser with a Data->Control seam: evade it, poison its training, prompt-inject the AI analyst's incident summary. Offense gap = wherever defense automated faster than it hardened.

frontier_seams.name: Capability-Composition
frontier_seams.summary: Skills, plugins, containers compose at runtime, each inheriting host authority. Composition unions authority but not scrutiny: as exposed as the least-vetted component, as trusting as the most-vetted.

coverage.$schema: Drives the non-deletion invariant (NEW DOES NOT CANCEL OLD). Every classic mind-map branch and every ATT&CK Enterprise tactic must resolve to >=1 seam in seams.json, or be explicitly logged honest-na here. validate.ts fails the build if any classic technique is silently dropped. honest_na_cells declare permanent matrix (principal x tactic) blanks so the typing test never renders a plain blank.

branch_honest_na.reason: Reporting is the red-team operator's own deliverable phase. It violates no victim trust relationship, so it has no seam. Drawn honest-na deliberately, not left blank.

honest_na_cells.reason: Active Directory has no command-and-control channel of its own; C2 rides the host/network. Permanent honest-na.
AD is not an egress; exfiltration leaves via host/network, not the directory service. Permanent.
A passive retrieval index does not beacon. C2 that abuses it is modeled as memory-as-dead-drop on the agent, not the store. Permanent.
An index has no destructive impact surface of its own; impact lands on the agent/host it feeds. Permanent.
A document is an ingress conduit, not a control channel. Permanent.
A file conduit holds no credentials to access; credential access targets stores, not the conduit. Permanent.
The messaging conduit carries impact payloads but is not itself the impacted resource. Permanent.
The fetch conduit is a delivery/ingress surface, not a destruction target. Permanent.

register.rationale: Tool approval in MCP is a point-in-time trust act, but the broker re-fetches tool definitions on reconnect. Nothing binds the approved tool semantics to a hash/version, so a server can alter a tool's description or backend behavior after consent and inherit it (P3 provenance: the name is trusted, the artifact changed). This is dependency-confusion's cousin living inside the agent's tool registry, decomposed out of the §3 Tool-Broker seam.
register.suggested_research: Pin and diff MCP tool manifests across reconnects; measure how many popular clients re-prompt on description/schema change vs silently accept. Build a 'tool manifest TOFU + change-alert' shim and quantify drift in the wild.

register.rationale: RAG poisoning is usually framed as a one-shot write, but indexes have capacity, recency weighting, and eviction. An attacker who understands the embedding space can flood near-duplicate vectors to (a) evict benign neighbors and (b) keep their payload in the top-k for chosen queries across time. That converts a P3 write into a P6 time/state foothold — the poisoned chunk stays true-at-use long after the write. Named but undecomposed in §3's retrieval seam.
register.suggested_research: Model eviction policies (LRU, similarity-capped, TTL) as an adversarial game; measure dwell-time of an injected chunk under realistic write pressure; design eviction that is provenance-weighted, not purely recency/similarity.

register.rationale: Long-term memory is increasingly a managed multi-tenant service. If the namespace key is derived from a spoofable attribute (display name, email alias, thread id) or the vector backend is shared without a hard tenant filter, retrieval can pull a neighbor's memory. This is a P4 context-inheritance leak across a tenant boundary that nobody re-validates at read time. §3 names memory-as-dead-drop but treats it single-tenant.
register.suggested_research: Audit hosted agent-memory products for tenant-key derivation and retrieval-time filtering; attempt similarity-collision retrieval across tenants; propose mandatory tenant-scoped ANN partitions with read-time authz.

register.rationale: As agent meshes adopt A2A-style protocols they introduce task/capability tokens. Early designs frequently lack signing, audience binding, or replay protection — the same mistakes Golden SAML exploited, reborn between machines. A forged token lets a rogue worker impersonate the orchestrator (or a trusted peer) and inject authoritative tasking. This is the P3/P2 sibling of §3's inter-agent seam, which only covered persuasion, not identity forgery.
register.suggested_research: Survey emerging A2A/agent-protocol token schemes for signature, audience, and nonce handling; build a forging harness; push for mandatory audience-bound, short-lived, signed task tokens with replay caches.

register.rationale: Modern SOC pipelines retrain or auto-tune on analyst feedback. That feedback is a trust input across time (P6): an attacker can patiently generate activity that gets dispositioned benign, conditioning the model/allowlist, then execute the real attack using the now-trusted pattern. This is slower and more insidious than one-shot poisoning and is unmentioned in §3, which only lists evade/poison/inject.
register.suggested_research: Instrument a feedback-trained detector; measure how few FP-closures are needed to suppress a signature; design feedback hygiene (provenance-tagged dispositions, decay, adversarial-aware re-weighting).

register.rationale: Agents increasingly install skills/plugins by name from registries with little vetting. Dependency confusion and typosquatting transfer directly, but the blast radius is worse: the installed skill runs with composed agent+host authority and can write the agent's memory for persistence. This is the explicit §5.2 'supply-chain x agent-skill typosquat-at-install' diagonal, decomposed from the capability-composition seam.
register.suggested_research: Crawl emerging agent-skill registries for namespace-squatting exposure; test resolver precedence (public vs private); propose signed, namespaced, install-time-reviewed skill manifests.

register.rationale: The (memory x cloud) diagonal under P3 extends classic subdomain/S3 takeover into agent memory. Cloud names are reusable; a remembered bucket/role/endpoint can be re-registered by an attacker after deletion. An agent that trusts its recollection instead of re-resolving ownership will write secrets to, or take tasking from, an attacker-owned resource. The seed never crosses memory with cloud naming.
register.suggested_research: Test agents for re-validation of remembered ARNs/buckets before use; catalog dangling-resource reuse windows; design memory that stores ownership proofs (account id + creation token), not bare names.

register.rationale: The (agent x container) diagonal under P2 generalizes the §3 machine-speed seam to Kubernetes. Ops teams are wiring agents to ServiceAccounts for autoremediation. RBAC roles like edit/admin were calibrated for cautious humans; an injected agent exercises them continuously and without judgment, and in-cluster they compose with container-escape primitives. Unpopulated in the seed.
register.suggested_research: Survey agent-on-k8s patterns for SA scope; measure blast radius of edit-bound agent under injection; propose intent-bound, just-in-time, human-rate-limited cluster RBAC for non-human principals.

register.rationale: The (human x memory) diagonal under P6 is a TOCTOU the seed misses. Humans assume corrections are authoritative and permanent, but memory subsystems summarize, deduplicate, and rank — a retracted instruction can survive as a high-salience entry and resurface sessions later. The gap between 'what the user last meant' and 'what the agent stored' is a time/state seam an attacker can deliberately widen.
register.suggested_research: Test whether agents honor retractions across summarization/compaction; build a 'retraction persistence' benchmark; design memory with explicit supersession and decay semantics for corrected facts.

register.rationale: Identity & Federation is a peer node bridging every substrate, and agents now sit between the human and the backends. The (identity x agent) diagonal under P2 is unpopulated in the seed. Token-exchange (RFC 8693) and OBO flows are being bolted onto agent stacks fast; if audience/act claims aren't enforced, the agent becomes a universal confused deputy at the identity layer. Classic Golden-SAML logic, new position.
register.suggested_research: Audit agent frameworks' token-exchange usage for audience/actor-claim enforcement; build a downstream that wrongly trusts issuer-only; propose mandatory act-claim + audience pinning for agent OBO.

register.rationale: Every new transport reintroduces P5. MCP added stdio/SSE/streamable-HTTP framing and JSON-RPC batching on top of HTTP proxies — exactly the conditions that produced HTTP request smuggling. The (mcp x network) diagonal under P5 is empty in the seed, yet brokers, gateways, and reverse proxies now sit in front of MCP servers. A framing desync could smuggle or split tool calls below the agent's visibility.
register.suggested_research: Differential-test MCP gateways/proxies for CL/TE-style and batch-boundary ambiguities; build a smuggling probe for streamable-HTTP MCP; propose canonical framing + length-prefixed batching.

register.rationale: The (memory x supplychain) diagonal joins two frontier nodes the seed never crosses. Agents that 'remember' which package/endpoint worked last time create a durable, low-scrutiny provenance store. An attacker who lands one poisoned memory write converts it into repeated supply-chain redirection, because the agent trusts its own recollection over a fresh resolution. Classic dependency confusion, persisted in the agent's head.
register.suggested_research: Test whether popular agents re-validate remembered endpoints/packages against the registry each run; design memory entries that carry verifiable provenance and force re-resolution for security-relevant facts.

register.rationale: Crossing the retrieval seam (F4) with the detection-model seam (F7) yields an unenumerated (vectorstore x soc) diagonal: defenders are deploying RAG copilots over corpora that include externally-writable data (customer tickets, alert annotations, threat-intel feeds). The write side is open to the very adversary the read side is defending against. Neither §3 seam covers the defender-as-RAG-consumer case.
register.suggested_research: Enumerate which SOC-copilot corpora are externally writable; demonstrate triage-suppression via planted ticket text; propose provenance-segregated retrieval (defender-authored vs externally-sourced) with trust labels in-context.

register.rationale: The (vectorstore x vectorstore) self-diagonal under P4 captures a deployment reality: teams consolidate on one managed vector DB and separate consumers only by metadata filter, not by isolation. A missing or bypassable filter lets one agent's ingested context be retrieved by another, an inheritance the seed's single-index framing doesn't anticipate. Sibling to memory cross-tenant bleed but at the index layer.
register.suggested_research: Audit managed vector DBs for filter-bypass via ANN recall vs post-filter ordering; demonstrate cross-consumer retrieval; push for physical namespace isolation over metadata-only filtering.
